speaker: interviewer
text: You left Krakow with your sister and went--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Otwock.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Otwock.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they lived there for a little while, but my brother-in-law Marek was very smart. He knew that the Russians will close the borders and will not let us across anywhere. So he said, after I went to school that time-- and I didn't want to go to sixth grade because I had five grades, and I wanted to go on. And I said to my sister, take me to private teacher. I'll do it faster because that's what we did in the ghetto. But she says, no no, you, you were working. You have to feel like a child again. You have to go to school with children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went to school with children. And of course, I always read ahead. And I knew all the answers. And the Polish kids were very jealous. So one day, when I went outside to-- we went out to play, there was a Polish boy that called me dirty Jew. And to me, dirty Jew was-- after the war, that's impossible, dirty Jew. So I fought with him. I broke my finger. You know, I punched him so hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I want to tell you that I wanted to be a pianist. That stopped it. That was my dream and my goal. And we-- so I-- they put on a sling. But you know, being almost 14 years old, I, I washed myself and took off the sling, which wasn't the right thing to do. So it didn't heal properly. So now, when I play fast, it slips.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like to be back there, after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very sad because we didn't go to the house. I just went once to see, and we cried. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we stayed away. And we were there a few short weeks because my brother in law looked for a way to get out, where to go in order to be closer to the American zone. So he said, let's go to Breslau. Breslau was a German port. But the Polish people took it over because it used to belong long once to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there again a few weeks. And in Breslau, we rented an apartment with a piano. Aha! There is my dream coming through. And I started taking, then, piano lessons, and I really worked very hard. So then Rachel came. And Rachel was going to take me to Hungary, where Lonya was with her son and with her husband. And we were already at-- on the train station, and the train was canceled because of the armies. And we slept the whole night on the floor. And then in the morning, she says you know what? You better not come with me. Go back to Tofka and stay with her. So I went back to Tofka. You see, she was the most educated from all the sisters. And, and that's where I got my education, because of them. They pushed it. So they didn't mind paying for the piano lessons, even though they didn't have much money. So where did go from Breslau?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Breslau, my brother-in-law, again, found a place. He said, when we go to Erfurt, to Germany, it was the-- it's the-- it's like the city of flowers. It was all Russians zone. We're closer to the American zone. And maybe we'll find a way to get there. So we got to know a captain there. And we kept company with the captain. With another, I don't know his name, but the boy did visit as lon-- a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sioma Finkelstein, he came to us a lot. He knew Hebrew very well. And one day, after he got to know us, he said to my brother-in-law, do you think you can help me escape the Russian army? He says, sure, I will. But I will not put the clothes in the building where we are. I'll put it someplace else under the steps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what happened. We gave him money and clothes. And we told him to-- how to get to the American zone. And his aim was to go to Israel. I tried to find him, but I couldn't. Maybe he didn't get to Israel. That's possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there also a few weeks. I'm telling you, this is all-- years, it seems. But it wasn't. We went to-- he found out, my brother-in-law, that we're going to take an excursion. And in one truck was the-- it wasn't just us. It was a whole group, the clothes and everything. And on the bus, the people were sitting. But since [BACKGROUND NOISE] we were always afraid of the-- of hunger, so all we could think of is to take the food with us-- the fat and the flour. That's most important, the oil. And the rest was in the truck. So when we came to, to the Russian end of the zone, when they said, where are you going? Oh we're going on a [NON-ENGLISH], on an excursion. Where are you going? I don't remember even where my brother in law said and the bus driver said. He said, OK, but we have to inspect the baggage. We know where you going. So we'll send it to you. And we lost everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just [LAUGHS] we had-- I had a pail of fat. My sister had a bundle of flour. I mean, that was important to us. And we lost all the pictures that the Polish people gave us that we had. The pictures I have is only from Israel, from my sister Malka. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, we went to Zeilsheim in the displaced persons camp on the American zone. And there it was very good because [INTERPOSING VOICES] the American had a kinderheim, but I stayed with my sister. And they were schools. There was the ORT. There was a Hebrew day school. And there wasn't a high school yet. So I didn't know any Hebrew because it looks like I threw it out to forget. So I didn't know anything-- not Yiddish, and not Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I came there, I had to go to first grade. Well, some kids were small and some kids were bigger. But I wasn't ashamed. I have here a friend in Pasadena that-- she's my age. But she would never go to a first grade with little kids. So I went there. And I went to day school and night school. So when I finished all the alphabet in the night school, and I knew how to read, they keep pushing-- kept pushing me on. And then I caught on very easily.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I went to gymnasium. It was-- it was called [NON-ENGLISH], which was-- the kids were then already 16 going on-- 16, I think, or no, maybe-- '45, '46-- so they were 17, 18. I was younger because my friends were older than me. And I studied very hard. We studied Latin and Polish and Hebrew. In Hebrew, there was no interpretation. As a matter of fact, we had to talk and write. And for every word we used not in Hebrew, he would tell us to, to give a penny to tsdoke, to charity. And we had to keep our own chart, how much he trusted us. So that was forced learning a language, in the same language, not to translate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed there till I finished-- you see, we studied six days a week. So our pace was accelerated. And we-- I finished high school in the summer. And I studied piano there too. But my-- in the meantime, my brother-in-law worked for the evil. He collected from the Germans all kinds of papers, pictures, artifacts. They trusted him very much. He was small. He wasn't athletic. And he had a high forehead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the German-- he knew German. So the Germans trusted him, and they gave him all the things. So because he did such a good job, they gave us an apartment in Hochst, which was, at Zeilsheim, was about half an hour walking. So I had to walk to school every day back and forth because there was no transportation. And it was many times very embarrassing because there were taxis that Jewish people bought, and they would stop and take me. And I said, I'm sorry. I have no money. But they would take me anyways.

speaker: interviewee
text: The two brothers were here. They died since, the Weiss brothers. And so it was funny that the weather is just like it now too. I have to laugh. When they predict rain, there is no rain. When they don't, there is. So I was going with an umbrella. They predicted rain. There wasn't. And when I didn't take it, it was. Half an hour was a long way to go. I wanted a bicycle, but my sister wouldn't let me. She says I'll might run into a post. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was studying piano as well. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And my sister, Tofka, had a little baby. And the carriage was-- we lived in an apartment with Germans. And I knew you could sense that they hate us. So we used to leave the carriage under the steps, instead of taking it up on the second floor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day after summer-- I mean during the summer, when I finished high school, I didn't know what I want to do. My sister wanted to be-- be an x-ray technician. And I didn't want to do anything with sick people. So I said no, I don't want that. The pianist, I couldn't be. So I didn't know what I wanted to do-- a journalist. I wanted to write.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I used to be-- there was a river across the road from Hochst which was called Main River. And I used to take my nephew to, to the-- near the river to-- the air was better there-- to sleep or to eat, or just to play. And one day, I come down to the carriage, and you can imagine my horror. Here I hold the little boy. He's about 18 or maybe 18, 19 months. And the carriage is broken. Somebody came and bashed the thing from all sides. So I ran up crying to my sister. I said, I don't want to be here anymore. If you want to stay here, then you can. I'm going to the orphanage. And wherever they send me, it's OK with me. I said, if you'd be registered someplace-- they didn't want to go to the United States. And they didn't want to go to Israel because my [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] brother-in-law bought all machineries to open a mill in Israel, which was the wrong thing to do. And he went and spoke to Ben Gurion. And Ben Gurion said sure, you can bring it. But I'll be a bigger partner than you. I'll have 51%. You can have the rest. He says, how can you do that? You don't know anything about the mill. I'm the expert. And I bring the machinery. And you're going to be a bigger partner? Did you want to go to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I really wanted to go to Israel. But my sister said, you cannot go with Aliyah Bet, which means illegally, because you were not in a camp, actually work camp. And they'll catch you. You'll go to Cypress. You'll be like a camp-- in a camp again. She says, no I don't allow you to. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So where was the orphanage?

speaker: interviewee
text: The orphanage was near Heidelberg in Aglasterhausen. It was an orphanage not just for Jewish children. It was an orphanage for all kinds of children-- Polish, Ukrainian, Russian. And I went there. And since there was no school for me because I finished high school, so I worked in the library. But in the mornings, I always had to go to the orphanage to dress the kids, wash them, feed them. And then I went to the library.

speaker: interviewer
text: and And where-- how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well I stayed there from summer, till the beginning of November. They called me into the office that they want to send me to the United States as a domestic. I said, I don't believe that. I worked as a domestic during the year I finished high school. You want me to go as a domestic? I ran out crying. I didn't want to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they called me in in November-- the end of November, or the middle I think. A group of children are going to the con-- to the Canadian consul. Do you want to go to Canada? I said, sure. I went to-- so we went to the consul, and we-- I passed because I want to come back a little bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: After I came from Krakow to home, to the dry air, I didn't need any injections. The cough went away like with a hand. So they, they took us to Italy, a group of children-- I don't know, 50. It was the last transport of the Canadian-- that the Canadian Congress took care of. That was, I was in the last. As a matter of fact there are two children from Aglasterhausen that I still write with because when we went on the boat, we stopped in Halifax. We embarked-- I mean disembarked and we stayed there a day in a synagogue. We slept on the floor. And then they decided to leave a few kids in Halifax. And they didn't-- one thing they did good, that they never separated cousins or sisters. But like this, they would pick anyone they wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: How had it felt to say goodbye to your sister after--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll tell you. It was very sad, especially to the sister that I was with. Rachel was then in Munich, in Munchen, and I said goodbye to her too. I was so happy to leave Germany that it overtook any other emotions because I just couldn't stay there. I mean, we had a telephone in-- in Hochst, but who am I going to call, the German police? You see what I mean? I just hate--

speaker: interviewer
text: They used to go to the show a lot, my sister and my brother-in-law and leave me with the baby. And I was so petrified because the door, like this, on the second floor was all glass. How much would it take to break it and get in? I was petrified. I just didn't like it.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in Zeilsheim, it wasn't as bad because there were lots of Jewish people. But when we moved to Hochst, I just-- I-- all my thoughts were with schooling. But once the schooling was finished, I hated to be there. So they-- every station, like in Halifax, they picked a few kids to stay. And you never knew if they pick you. Then the next step was Montreal. And they picked another few kids to stay. Then next stop was Toronto. And they picked me. And my husband happened to be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know him already?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew him from Zeilsheim. So they picked me. And they-- there was a counselor. And she said to me, what do you want to do? I said, I want to be a journalist. She says, you are too old for that. I said, me? I'm not yet 18 years old. I finished the gymnasium, which is like two years of college here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you want me to-- what do you want me to do? She says, well, we'll send you to work, and you'll be a mother's helper. Work when she said, mother's helper, I ran out crying and slammed the door. I think I broke it. And I never saw a counselor again. But I went to live with a family that I worked nearby. I walk to work. And I made $18 a week. And guess what? I was a mother's helper. [DRINKS] And when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why I got married because I'll tell you. I was very naive. Here are my two sisters sending a young girl to the world. I never read any books because it was in German, see? During the war, I didn't. So I was very naive. And here, there are lots of boys, put the hands on. Put-- oh my God, what am I going to? At least the others didn't know my sister. Him, I thought he would respect me because he knew my sister and my brother-in-law. So I felt more sure with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Alan Greenstein. It was then Abramich they called him. Or when he came to Canada before me, they gave him a name, Alan. So he-- so he's Alan to this day. And I came in the end of November. And in March, I got married. So what I want to tell you that this woman had a young son. And it was like here, there was tabloid or whatever those sensational-- it was all written up that he raped a young girl. He went to jail. And I was living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So his cousin told him, he says, no way is she going to stay there. I came to live with him in the attic. And soon after, we got married. So-- How is it that you think you, as such a young girl, were able to live by your wits and survive all of the different places that you stayed in when you were completely by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well somebody else asked me, when I came here to Los Angeles the same question. And I can only say that I had a very sound childhood. It's just like you shoot up the-- an Atlantis or a missile. It has a very good foundation. And that's what I think. I got good teaching, from-- especially from my father. Because you can imagine my mother worked very hard. She hardly had time. But my father gave a lot of time. I used to walk with him at night. We had a very long backyard. And there was a light in between, like a streetlight. And he was telling me stories and this and that. And a lot of it went to my head. Even though it was buried, but somehow when I need, it came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you find that it made it easier for you to adjust to being alone in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was just a few short months in Canada when I met him, and we decided to get married before Passover. I wanted to get married the first day of spring, but it came out Sunday was the 20th. So I was married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you live in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: 15 years. I had two children, Gloria and Joseph, Joey. And when Joey was two years old, I decided to go to the seminary for teachers. When he came from work-- we had one car-- I left for the seminar.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you became a teacher?

speaker: interviewee
text: I became in Canada. It took me two years. And it's a funny thing that the rabbi that taught us Bible, he, he kept asking me when I took him home every night, I can't understand it, he says. You had such a short education. And you went to third grade of the Midrashah, and my daughter went to nursery since she was young. And they gave her a test and she went to first grade.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of teachers did you have? What kind of books did you have? I said we didn't have books. And the teachers, I don't know if they all were legitimate teachers. But it looks like it's up to the person, really. Did these people know what you had experienced during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't talk about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- there, there was no occasion. The only occasion was when I came the first day to the seminary. I always, even now when I go someplace, I don't hide in the back. I go in the front. And here I sit in the front, and the rabbi sits with the desk. And he calls the names. And I don't respond to any. He said, are you registered. I said, sure. I'm waiting a few years for the school to open.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, but I called all the names. Well I'll do it once more. And he calls, and I'm a G. So I come early. Greenstein, that's me. He said, but I called your name before. How come you didn't respond? And I knew right away why. He had blue eyes like my father, just from here. He was a very tall person, respectable. And I was a Kirszenbaum. I wasn't a Greenstein. So I really didn't hear my name. So at recess time, I caught myself why I didn't respond the first time. So at recess time, we went out in the hallway. He was smoking, Rabbi Shochet. He has a son here and a daughter in Los Angeles. I told him the story, and he became my best friend then. Now I know you have some photographs to show, but before we do that, do you have a final message that you would like to give to the people who will be seeing this tape in the future?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's to survive-- the survival is a very strong drive. And maybe because of the speech that my father gave to us, before we left from the ghetto that we must survive. We must do the commandments. He cannot go because he did the commandments. So life is really very precious. And that's how I was brought up. That you don't kill animals. You don't kill even insects. And you should see me on Saturday. A fly is here. I'll catch it by hand and throw it out the door. Don't even kill it. I mean, if it doesn't bother you, you don't kill it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's what it is. Life must go on, no matter what. And that's to get married, and to have children, and to show the Germans that you wanted to kill us all. But see? You did not succeed. [INAUDIBLE] Unfortunately, the German succeeded a lot. Six million is not just a few. But those that remain, and they got married, and they try to have a normal life as much as possible. But the pain is still here. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was just in the early 50s. So was my mother. And I'm 64 and a half. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So in the bedroom, I surrounded them, with the grandchildren. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK we'll look at those pictures now.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the picture of my family on Purim in 1934, before my sister Malka went to Israel. I'll start with the youngest. That's me there, with the bow on the head.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: The left. And the next is my sister, Sarah. Next to her is my sister, Naomi. My sister, Tofka, is sitting between my brother, Yitzchak, that lives in Canada. My father's in the middle, with the cup. I have the cup in my-- actually I gave it to my daughter because she's Orthodox. So they have it. Then is my brother on the other side of my father. To the right is [NON-ENGLISH], my mother, who is standing up. Then next to my mother sits Lonya, the oldest. The next oldest is Malka. She went away to get married in Palestine that time. And then is Rachel, the last one.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is you with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon? This picture was taken winter 1934. My sister Rachel always was writing and sending pictures to my sister Malka in Palestine. That's how we have the pictures. I'm here below. I was five years old. And this is my sister Rachel that lives in Saint Paul, Minnesota in the winter. This picture was taken after the war, in 1946. My sister took it in Munchen when I came to visit her. I mean she had it made by a photographer.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is it a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a picture of me, when I was 15, 15 years old. I still have my braids. This picture was taken in the orphanage, in Aglasterhausen near Munchen. There were lots of other children, not just Jewish. And I am the third from the right. I was there a few months until I went to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- what year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1948. This is my wedding picture, March 20, 1949 in Toronto, Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the groom?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband Alan Greenstein that they knew from Zeilsheim, Germany. This picture was taken in 1967.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Toronto, Canada, at my brother's son's bar mitzvah. These are the remaining sisters and one brother. There is Tofka from the left, and Malka from Israel came. Lonya came from New York. Of course, my brother Yitzchak was in Toronto. Rachel came from St. Paul, Minnesota. And I came from Los Angeles, like real Jews. This picture was taken at my son's bar mitzvah, at the Hollywood Temple Beth El. It was right after my nephew's bar mitzvah from Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the picture is my husband Alan, and Gloria, my daughter, my son, Joey, the bar mitzvah boy, and myself. The three boys are my son's children. There's the oldest one, Jonathan nine years old. Jeffrey is eight. And Daniel is six.

speaker: interviewer
text: Say who she is and--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my only granddaughter, Lisa Beth Eisman. They live in Riverdale, New York. This is my-- her brother, Lisa Beth's brother, my grandson, Nathaniel Eisman. And he's going to be bar mitzvahed next year in Israel on top of Masada.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this picture was taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken last year in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Actually it was taken in 1992.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was, '92? Oh boy. See, you know better. Yeah, see 1992. He's a big boy.
